speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 4 of an interview with Masha Friedman-Zeleznikow. At the end of the last tape you mentioned that you had an ambition to study and you were able to obtain a scholarship. Can you please tell me a bit more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So when-- how did it come that I decided-- you know, actual people ask me how could you, after such a terrible war, and after such terrible things, how could you wanted to stay on in Poland and study? But, you know, when you are young, you don't think like that. I knew that we have struggled years in Siberia and later on, years in [INAUDIBLE]. And I wanted to become something. And I knew that if I will not stay on in Poland, I knew that if I will move, I will lose the language. And it was hard enough as it was, because, you've got to realize, one has to realize, how many languages I went through. I came to Russia without a word of Russian. And then when I came out of Russia, I forgot Polish, because Polish, although it was my number one language-- but if you don't speak a language for five, six years, you completely-- so you lose track. And when I came back to Poland, there were no Polish books. 1945, when we came to Katowice, you couldn't find a Polish book. And I started to read German.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for one year, I read only German books. And I then-- I thought to myself, if I have again now to lose and start with a new language, then I really won't be able to do it, because I was thinking, at that stage, that I'm getting old and I have to the something in order to finish my studies. And as it happened, 1946 was the year, really, that I blossomed. All of us, the ones that came back, because Poland was-- we would get parcels from-- from United States. And for the first time, my mother in Russia, my mother always promised me that when we'll come to a city-- all these years in Russia, I didn't have one single thing, nothing-- nothing new. I mean, whatever I had, we would wash and wear again. So she always used to promise me, when we will come to a big city, you'll get a new dress. And 1946 was the year, really that we got a few new dresses, and I was-- How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 18. And I-- that was for me-- even when I think back, that was for me a very-- a time where I was very happy. Regardless, I mean, I've seen my parents being terribly unhappy, but I didn't think much about it, you know. I wanted to study. And so-- and then, also, I for the first time, that I had a very lovely group of young people. Because in 1945, as I said, people were coming back from concentration camps, young people. And the ones that decided to stay on in Poland were the ones that wanted to study.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I became friendly with these people. I also had a few people that came back from Russia. And I had a very nice group of people. And so I felt quite happy. And at the same stage, at the same time, the bund started to organize. And suddenly, they made a camp-- a youth camp. This was 1946, June or July 1946. And we were invited to that camp. And I went with my brother and my sister and another few people from Katowice. And this is where I, for the first time, met my husband. It took many years, still, but at least-- so this was like a beginning. My husband was in Lodz already by that time. And they said to me-- people that came from Lodz said to me, why don't you come to Lodz? Because Katowice didn't have a university. Well, actually, the only university apart from Krakow was Lodz. So this is when I decided, against my father's wishes-- my father didn't speak to me for a year after that-- but I decided that I'm going to Lodz to study.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1946, after that camp, I went to Lodz. And there was a conference from bund. And so I also started-- the first few months-- this was, of course like-- I did not recognize-- although I came top and I had a Russian [INAUDIBLE], they didn't recognize. You had to make-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. This was something that-- sort of like a course that entitled you an entry to university. And it took me a few months. And I came. I did very well. I even got like a-- I got a book. I came top of that year also. I got special recognition. Was what's the book, that later on, I brought Australia. It was [INAUDIBLE] 'The War of Jews,' a very famous book. And after that, I started university. And those were very happy years in Lodz. My parents, although they were not very happy with what I did, because they were thinking that I should go to work and help them. And-- so-- but they acknowledged it. And they had to make peace with it. The only problem was that, in Lodz, I had a few times-- those were the times where there was a lot of Polish bands. And it was called NSZ and people Armia Krajowe, this was the army-- not people's army. People's army was the Russian-- AK, the were very anti-Semitic. And I had a few times that I had to travel when it was a holiday or something, I would go back home to Katowice. And I had a few times, the people were taken off the train and shot. You know, so-- You constantly had it. You had the Germans or you had the Russian and then-- then you had the Poles that were very anti-Semitic. Although, in Lodz, we had a lot of Poles at the university. But this was a different crowd, you know. They were people that were mostly-- either they were pro-Jewish or pro-communist. And they were fighting among themselves, so always the Jew was the scapegoat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you studying in university?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was studying medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you mentioned before that you met your husband for the first time.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name is Abram Zeleznikov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what was your first impression of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my first impression, when I first met at that youth camp, we were all laughing, because he was really-- they called him the partisan. If one remembers that this was 1946, and the war finished, he came out of the partisans in 1945. So it was only a year from that. And he was-- he was very-- he was a very moody man, which he stayed on up to now. You know, he could be in a wonderful mood and then he would go very angry. And he was full of anger. But he was very young, I don't remember. I think he was 20 at that stage. Yeah, he was born-- he was 21. And-- but we're only friends at that time. I mean, I did not-- nothing was further from my mind than marriage. And I wanted to study. And I wanted to stay on in Poland. But things did change bit by bit. Well, first of all-- I mean, the anti-Semitic in Poland didn't bother me so much, because I've encountered it, and I didn't like it. But it wasn't my everyday, you know. I was living in Lodz. Lodz had something which was called [POLISH]. This was like youth hostels, where everybody was studying. And everybody was full of-- you know, young people, everybody wanted to study. And everybody was wonderful. And they all had scholarships. And they were all helped a bit by the parcels that would come from United States. And it was a wonderful atmosphere. And, you know I didn't want to think beyond that. When-- so this was 1947-- 1946 part 1947. In 1948, the situation changed. The situation changed for the worse in political. What happened, my father was a Bundist. And my husband is a Bundist. And the Bund, which was the Jewish socialists, started to feel very uncomfortable with the political situation in Poland. The Communist Party united in 1948. And that was like signal for every Bundist that they have to run for their life. Because what happens, the moment they united, the same situation happened in every country where the Communists took power. Because the moment they united, the Communists were the top, and everybody else would go to jail. And my father started pressing and started to nag me that he wants to leave for Paris. My brother at that stage was in Paris already. My brother, which was 17 only, went to Paris earlier. My parents told him they wanted him to see how the situation is in Paris, how he can cope. And later on, we will come. I was very unhappy with it. I said, I will not move. Because, I knew again that I'm going to lose everything. I had friends, and I was going to have a profession. And I knew that by moving, I will lose. And I didn't want to go. But-- in-- in meantime, I started to go out with my husband more serious. And my husband had to run away. And already, he did not get a permission. He had to run illegally. He had to smuggle already with a few friends. And I realized that whether I like it or not, I have to go. So I've decided that I will go. And then my parents made some plans. And we left everything. My uncle came back from Russia. One of my uncles that went to Russia came back from Russia, and he was a Communist, he stayed on. And he took over our flat. And we were-- we went-- we took both the ticket, and we went. And what did happen on the Czech border, in the middle of the night, again, KGB came, it wasn't KGB, it was UB, which was the Polish, Bezpieczenstwa, Polish KGB. And they took off my father. And they didn't say, you know-- they don't give any-- it's like Russia. They didn't tell you why [INAUDIBLE], they just took off. And they said, you can go. But we decided that we won't go without him. It was very traumatic, you know. I felt guilty that maybe if we would have gone earlier maybe that would not have happened. But finally, we went back, the whole family-- my mother, my sister, myself, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother, where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in Paris already by that time. He was a year in Paris. He went 1947. And we went back. And we didn't have a house where to live, we stayed with some friends, which came later on to Australia. It was a very sad situation, because we had nothing again. My father was in prison. And also luggage. I had special permission to take out 400 books, you know. I put in a lot of work to get that special permission from the Ministry of Culture-- Ministry of Culture. Because they wouldn't let out, you know-- I had some wonderful books, which I wanted to take with me. And we had some furniture. Everything went to Paris, and we went back to Poland. What we tried-- what I tried to do was to see my father in prison and find out-- have some-- what to do. And a few weeks I was struggling, and finally we-- I got a permission to see him. And the first thing that he said to me is, take you mother and go to Paris, because if you go to Paris, I have a chance. I have no documents. I didn't do anything that I shouldn't. And this is just a political thing. But if you go to Paris, there is a hope that I will be able to get out, that they will release me. If you stay on here, I'll never get out, and you will disappear. So he said go to Paris. All right, so that was it. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did the family decide on Paris? Because your brother was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why we decided-- no. Because Paris was-- my brother went because of that. Because Paris-- some people went-- some people stayed in Germany. But most migrants or a lot of migrants from camps went to Paris. You know, you couldn't stay on in Paris for long. The French were not very sympathetic. And the time that we had in Paris was very ugly. The French were not nice at all. But why? Because nobody wanted you, you know. Once the war finished, you could think that with all the problems that they gave to the Jews, that the place will be-- that the world will be a better place, but it wasn't. You were unwanted wherever you came. And Paris was only a spot where you had to migrate. So we intended-- we had two places in mind. Either Canada or Australia, whichever, you know-- you needed a visa. And we couldn't sit in Poland to wait for that visa. So we picked Paris. It was just a matter-- and you couldn't go anywhere else. We didn't want Germany, you know. I've never set foot in Germany back. So we picked Paris because that was the only reasonable place we got a visa for a certain period. And we stayed on in Paris. Anyway, we came to Paris. And it was a very, again, a very terrible situation, because we came without money. We came without-- my mother was a very helpless woman always. We didn't know the language. And we were completely on the mercy of the Joint, you know. And we started to be migrant or refugee, whatever, all over again. And if I think about Paris of that time, it real-- I think was very-- very unpleasant feelings. Although, at certain times, oh, it wasn't so bad, because we-- we had-- we-- I joined-- I was in the Bund, in the youth movement. And we went to visit places with this youth group. But in reality, one felt, all the time, that one is a refugee. One felt unwanted. You had to go to the-- you had to-- every week, you had to tell the police-- tell them that you are alive. And my husband was beaten. The police were not nice. The French police were not nice at all. And so we were sitting and waiting. We didn't know what will happen to my father. We didn't know where-- we couldn't move anyway without-- so we were sitting, like and waiting on what's going to happen. Once again, from the start-- we had to start again from the beginning. At this point of time, I studied French. I went to [FRENCH] Francaise, because I thought I'll need a language. And I also went to the Ord. Ord was doing a wonderful job. They we're training people without-- without a trade, they were teaching. There were engineers. I, you know went for a dressmaking course. I never had a needle in my hand, I knew that I need some kind of-- I'll need something when we come to wherever. So I went and I finished. And it was very-- later on in life, I found it very useful, because I used-- when we came to Australia, I worked. In meantime, we were already-- my father-- one day, completely unexpected, my father turned up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not. We didn't know what happened to him. We didn't know where-- my mother was crying bitterly, you couldn't get her out of the room. We were living in a terrible, small-- you know, in Paris, in a hotel room. In a very cheap hotel. In one room. And we were sitting and waiting. And out of the blue, my father turned up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, this is the point. He went to the Joint, and he was told that we are-- we were living in a real [INAUDIBLE]. We had already, at that stage, we had already a little room. And-- and we were very happy. We started then to make plans, that either we go to Australia or we go to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship with Abram at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time we were still-- at that time, we started to go out in Poland already. And Abram told me, he started it, when he heard it that I took off my father. And that we went back to Poland, he was going-- he was ready to smuggle back into Poland to give us a hand, somehow, to get of Poland. But look, it would've been a disaster, because he would have been caught and put into jail. So Abram awaited us. When we came to Paris, you know-- look, we had lot of trouble with the books, with the luggage, everything got lost. Out of the few hundred books, only a few were left. And a lot of the luggage was left, because my brother didn't have enough money to pay for it. But still, we've survived. And once again we were together. And with Abram, we were planning to marry. And we didn't know how-- how to do it, because Abram got a job. I think Abram was the only one from thousands of refugees that got a job. Because he was a Yiddish teacher. And there was a lot of children from the Holocaust that were left. I don't know if he told you that, but was working as a teacher. And he was the only one from everybody that I knew. Because normally, you needed a permission, and nobody could get a permission. So people really couldn't work. And Abram was very lucky that he got a job. But it wasn't in Paris. It was in [FRENCH]. It was 50, 60 kilometers. It was for a home for, I think, underprivileged children, or maybe not underprivileged, maybe children that came out-- survived the war somehow. And he was teaching there. But just the same, we've decided that we will marry. We were planning to marry on Abram's birthday, which is the 25th of May. But we somehow, we didn't get a permission. It took us longer. And we married the 22nd October, which is my birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949. And we were still sitting and waiting for the visa. We still didn't know either we got to Australia or we go to Canada. We were prepared for one or the other. Whichever visa comes earlier. Although my father really wanted-- was thinking that Australia would be a better place, because it's far away. And we wanted as far away as possible. And then I became pregnant. And really, we were very poor. And when I became pregnant, my mother started to worry how will I manage, you know, thought that I'm not the kind of person that will be able to manage. And she needed a little operation, which we were not happy for her to go in Paris. We were thinking that we should go to Australia to have it done. It was a very small thing, and she went to a hospital. And after 24 hours, she was dead. She got a blood clot, an embolism, and died. And look, that was, for us, probably one of the most tragic moment, because we were just got out of such a war. And we've lost our mother, and she was 47, you know. So she was a very young woman. And so in a way my father felt that life was over for him. And we also felt very, very-- I felt terrible. Because, in a way, I felt that she wouldn't go for that operation if I wouldn't be pregnant. But that was it. We-- we stayed on. We still didn't have the visa, even. We stayed on. And one of the terrible things, which my daughter, later on, in later years-- when we came to Paris, my daughter was hysterical when she saw it. In, you know, Paris, they put 24 people in one grave. It's not that everybody has a single grave. So my mother was put in with 24 people, which is a terrible thing, you know. For us, we're not used to it. And-- and then we got the permits to go to Australia. And we decided then-- look, we didn't have much of a decision. We didn't have much that. But I had a little baby already by then. John was eight months. And the boat that we came was a boat that went once only. Because, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot already. That's right. It was a terrible boat. It was a ship, I think, for soldiers. You can't imagine the bad conditions that we had. It was a lot of people on that. And John got sick. And there was-- Penicillin was just new then. And the doctor-- the ship's doctor-- gave him constant injections of penicillin, and the child was really very, very sick. For us-- for me was a terrible journey that I never forgot. It took weeks and weeks, until we came to Colombo. And I remember then asking for a decent doctor. An Indian doctor came, and he said, look, there is really nothing wrong. Just give him proper food. Because he didn't have proper food. You know, there was no fresh milk or anything. And we had very bad conditions. And we came to Australia March, 1951. Which was-- we had a very hard time ahead of us. I realized it. First of all, there was no language, you know. No matter how many languages you spoke, there was no language. Then, there was no profession and no trade, because Abram never learned any trade. Although he also went to Ord, but he didn't learn a trade, you know. He was always the type-- a good speaker, but when it came to a trade, he wasn't so good. So the first years in Australia were very hard for us. We were-- we had completely no qualification to start a new life in a new country. And we were one week only in a house that Jewish welfare had somewhere in Camberwell. And then, we got a room in Thornbury. And-- you know, John was only 10 months when we came to Melbourne. And I couldn't work, which was really a disaster, because Abram could not make a living. He worked for big firms, like for small ones. It was an unemployment then. I mean, it was a lot of unemployed people. And although the people were saying that the times were very good, we couldn't see the good times, you know. There was no any social security. Now if a migrant doesn't work, he gets social security. There was no social security. If you didn't have money, or if you didn't have work, you didn't eat, simple as that. And I can tell you, that many times, we did not have what to eat. Those were the first years in Australia, which I think really was a very uncomfortable feeling. Then, if that's not enough, we came at a time-- I wasn't aware, but John got poliomyelitis. And at the age of two, he had got poliomyelitis. And I really think about those times. You know, we were not allowed to see him. It's unbelievable how Australia looked, how Melbourne was. Now, when you talk about it, people-- people don't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it? Your first impressions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'll tell you what happened, you know. We-- Yes. How did it happen. Well John used to get-- he used to get tonsillitis, the same way as I used to get them. And once, he got a tonsillitis. And because I couldn't speak English, I took a doctor that wasn't qualified, but spoke Yiddish. And she came, and she said, he's got tonsillitis, and nothing to worry. But then, I suddenly realized that he can't swallow. So I desperately rung to tell her that he can't swallow. The moment she heard that, she didn't want to come, because she obviously realized the he must be sick. And somebody said to me, just take a normal doctor. But we couldn't even speak to him. But finally, we did take somebody, which was very nice, and immediately realized that there was something seriously wrong. And he took us-- he took us with the child to the children's hospital. But what was terrible that I didn't-- the moment I realized-- the moment I [INAUDIBLE] test, and I realized that it's poliomyelitis, they put him to Fairfield, and you couldn't see him. So I went six weeks without being able to contact him. Each time I would ring, they would tell me either he's reasonable or he's not so good, you know. And I didn't know what it means even. Look, it was such a horrible thing that I asked friends to ring for me, and I thought maybe I don't understand. But the fact is, that was a terrible time, because you were-- you didn't-- you were not allowed to see your child. Which I don't think there was a country in the world where-- where-- where you couldn't see a little child that is sick. And there was another problem, that my husband wanted John always to speak only Yiddish. So the few words that John knew were only Yiddish. And so John couldn't communicate. And, although he wasn't speaking a lot, but he still could say something. And I really didn't know, either he's paralyzed, either he's can't speak. Either he can-- look, it was a terrible time. Until one day--

words.text: Yes, I'll tell you what happened,

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number 4.
